cards.front: Can you define a struct inside another struct?
cards.back: Yes. Nested structs are allowed and useful for types only relevant within the outer struct.

cards.question: How do you access the nested type?

cards.front: Can you assign one struct object to another of the same type?
cards.back: Yes. Memberwise assignment: each member is copied from source to destination.

cards.question: What are joe's values after this?

cards.front: Can you compare two structs with == operator by default?
cards.back: No. There's no default == for structs. You must overload operator== or compare members individually.

cards.text: The {{c1::sizeof}} operator returns the size of a struct in {{c2::bytes}}, including any {{c3::padding}}.

cards.front: Why might sizeof(struct) be larger than the sum of its members?
cards.back: Due to padding - the compiler adds extra bytes to align members for performance.

cards.question: Is this valid?
cards.answer: Yes. You can create arrays of struct objects.
cards.explanation: Structs can be used anywhere fundamental types can be used.

cards.front: Can you create temporary (anonymous) struct objects?
cards.back:
Yes. Using aggregate initialization without a variable name:
print(Employee{ 14, 32, 60000.0 });

cards.explanation:
Compilers add padding between/after struct members for alignment:
  • Improves performance on most architectures
  • Makes sizeof larger than sum of members
  • Depends on member order
  • Can use #pragma pack or alignas to control
examples: struct S1 { char c; int i; };  // Likely 8 bytes (padding)
struct S2 { int i; char c; };  // Same members, different size
sizeof(S1) may not equal sizeof(c) + sizeof(i)

cards.front: Where are struct definitions typically placed?
cards.back: In header files (.h) so they can be included and used across multiple source files.

cards.text: Struct assignment performs {{c1::memberwise copy}} of all members from source to destination.
sizeof(struct) includes the size of members plus any {{c1::padding}} bytes added for {{c2::alignment}}.
Structs can be compared member-wise only if you {{c1::overload operator==}}; there's {{c2::no default}} comparison.
Struct assignment performs {{c1::memberwise copy}} of all members from source to destination.
sizeof(struct) includes the size of members plus any {{c1::padding}} bytes added for {{c2::alignment}}.
